steps.title: Find the structure on each side of the blank
steps.content:
The sentence has two independent clauses separated by the conjunctive adverb “however.” The rule for joining two independent clauses with a conjunctive adverb is: IC; however, IC — a semicolon before and a comma after. Eliminate any choice that breaks this pattern.
  • Choice A — webs, however,: uses a comma before “however,” not a semicolon — a comma alone cannot join two independent clauses. ✗
  • Choice B — webs however,: omits all punctuation before “however” and places a comma after, violating the IC; however, IC rule. ✗
  • Choice C — webs, however;: reverses the required punctuation — the comma and semicolon are on the wrong sides of “however.” ✗
  • Choice D — webs; however,: places a semicolon before and a comma after “however,” exactly matching the IC; however, IC rule. ✓

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: The punctuation “webs; however,” correctly uses a semicolon before and a comma after “however” to join the two independent clauses. That confirms D is correct.
